coredata.dc:title: Dual-channel fluorescent signal readout strategy for cysteine sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Huajuan
Wu, Zitong
Zhao, Zexu
Zhu, Lin
Tang, Minggen
Yu, Ruijin
Wang, Jinyi
coredata.dc:description: Cysteine (Cys) is a biological thiol. Aberrant changes in thiol levels are associated with the development and pathogenesis of various diseases, including liver damage, Alzheimer's disease, weakness, and cardiovascular diseases. Therefore, thiol detection in biological samples has great importance in health monitoring and disease prediction. In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor combined with carbon dots (CDs)-doped mesoporous silica and fluorescein-based fluorescent probes loaded in pores for Cys detection. The nanosensor emitted fluorescence at 450 nm upon excitation at 370 nm. In the presence of Cys, the fluorescence emission from the probe could be selectively enhanced, whereas that from CDs could be changed. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed. This sensor can eliminate the potential influence of background fluorescence and other analyte-independent external environmental factors. The nanosensor was utilized to monitor Cys levels in human serum, and satisfactory results were obtained. Results indicated that the nanosensor can be utilized as an excellent fluorescent nanocomposite material in practical biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Ratiometric
Nanosensor
Cysteine
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cys in human serum
Characterization and optical properties of SiCDs
Detection of Cys in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21675126 and 21874108 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the SiCDs. (B) The corresponding size distribution histogram of the TEM image. (C) Absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (purple line), and emission spectra of the SiCDs. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of the SiCDs at different excitation wavelengths, inner picture: normalized fluorescence emission spectra. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
High-resolution XPS spectrum of the SiCDs: (A) full range, (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, (D) O 1s, (E) Si 2p. (F) FTIR spectra of the SiCDs.
TEM image (A) and size distribution histogram (B) of the SiCDs@MSN. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (C) and pore width distributions (D) of the SiCDs@MSN and the nanosensor.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the nanosensor upon the addition of various concentrations of Cys (from bottom to top: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 μM) in PBS solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM). (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of I518/I450 of the nanosensor and Cys concentration.
Fluorescence responses of the nanosensor toward analytes, including various amino acids, GSH, and Hcy.
(A) Strategy for synthesizing SiCDs and the nanosensor. (B) Mechanism of Cys detection.

head.ce:title: Dual-channel fluorescent signal readout strategy for cysteine sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Colleges of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Colleges of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cysteine (Cys) is a biological thiol. Aberrant changes in thiol levels are associated with the development and pathogenesis of various diseases, including liver damage, Alzheimer's disease, weakness, and cardiovascular diseases. Therefore, thiol detection in biological samples has great importance in health monitoring and disease prediction. In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor combined with carbon dots (CDs)-doped mesoporous silica and fluorescein-based fluorescent probes loaded in pores for Cys detection. The nanosensor emitted fluorescence at 450 nm upon excitation at 370 nm. In the presence of Cys, the fluorescence emission from the probe could be selectively enhanced, whereas that from CDs could be changed. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed. This sensor can eliminate the potential influence of background fluorescence and other analyte-independent external environmental factors. The nanosensor was utilized to monitor Cys levels in human serum, and satisfactory results were obtained. Results indicated that the nanosensor can be utilized as an excellent fluorescent nanocomposite material in practical biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have recently attracted considerable attention because of their excellent water solubility; resistance to photobleaching; high fluorescence quantum yield; low cytotoxicity; and good biocompatibility in various fields, such as bioimaging [1,2], catalysis [3], photodevices [4,5], sensing [6,7], and anti-fake labeling [8,9]. The current methods for synthesizing CDs can be divided into two types, namely, top–down [10,11] and bottom–up approaches [12–14]. Given the complex and harsh synthetic conditions of top–down approaches, bottom–up approaches have attracted greater attention because of their ease of handling and high productivity. Heteroatom doping can improve the fluorescence and surface physicochemical properties of CDs [15–19]. Thus, it has attracted the attention of a growing number of researchers.

Small biological thiols, a class of molecules that contain free thiol moieties, such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine, and glutathione (GSH), are essential in many physiological processes [20–22]. Cys deficiency is associated with growth retardation, hair fading, edema, lethargy, liver lesions, muscle loss, skin damage, and weakness. By contrast, elevated levels of Cys result in neurotoxicity and cardiovascular diseases [23,24]. For these reasons, thiol determination in biological samples has a great importance in health monitoring and disease prediction.

Up to now, the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [25], electrophoresis [26], electrochemistry [27], and mass spectrometry [28] have been applied for Cys detection. Most of these methods usually require professional staff, expensive instruments and involve cumbersome preconditioning procedures such as separation and purification of samples. Because of the high sensitivity, good selectivity, and easy operation, fluorescent probes for Cys detection have recently attracted more attention. However, most of the reported sensors that respond to Cys are single wavelength intensity modulation–type probes [29–33]. A single emission point is readily influenced by the presence of external factors, such as the solvent system, environmental temperature, concentration, excitation source, and nonuniform mixing of the receptor solution. The ratiometric fluorescence assay is an analytical method that measures the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths [34–38]. Given that the measured fluorescence ratio is unaffected by light source intensity, instrument sensitivity, and matrix background, this method has high sensitivity, selectivity, and a wide linear range.

In this study, we proposed a CDs-based ratiometric nanosensor for Cys detection (Scheme 1). The preparation and sensing mechanism of the ratiometric nanosensor are shown in Schemes A and B. First, SiCDs were prepared by using malic acid and N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO) via the hydrothermal method. Second, SiCDs were doped into mesoporous silicon. Lastly, the nanosensor was prepared by loading probes into the SiCDs-doped mesoporous silicon. The obtained nanosensor showed a peak at 450 nm. This peak could be assigned to the SiCDs emission. The nanosensor exhibited an emission peak at 518 nm during Cys addition. Background interference was effectively eliminated, and sensor sensitivity was improved by comparing the variations in the ratio of the two fluorescence intensities during sensing.

Malic acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO), acryloyl chloride and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). 5(6)-Carboxyfluorescein was procured from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium Bromide (CTAB) was purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Triethylamine, dichloromethane, methanol, and ethanol were supplied by Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All amino acids were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with Tecnai G2 Spirit Bio–Twin operated at 200 kV. All fluorescence spectra were recorded using an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-1780 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1 cm quartz cell. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo-VG Escalab 250 spectrometer (Thermo-VG Scientific, U.S.A.). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (BRUKER, Germany). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm was conducted by ASAP 2020 (Micromeritics, U.S.A.). All pH values were measured with a PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.). 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker 500 MHz spectrometer. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was performed using Waters e2695 instrument with a XAqua C18 5 μm 100 Å column and 2424 evaporative light-scattering detector.

SiCDs were prepared via the hydrothermal method. First, 54 mg of malic acid and 2 mL of DAMO were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the solutions were transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. Afterward, the solution was subjected to dialysis in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight, 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the SiCDs samples were obtained by freeze dying.

SiCDs doped mesoporous silicon nanoparticle (SiCDs@MSN) was synthesized by adding 0.5 mL of SiCDs (3 mg/mL) solution, 50 mg of CTAB, 4 mL of ethanol, 0.1 mL of NaOH solution (2 M), and 0.5 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate to 30 mL of water. The solution was heated at 45 °C under stirring for 6 h. After the reaction, the solution was centrifugated at 16,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the product. The surfactant (CTAB) was removed via the ion-exchange method. The SiCDs@MSN was transferred to 30 mL of ethanol that contained 0.5 g of NH4NO3, and the solution was maintained at 60 °C for 5 h [39,40]. This procedure was repeated twice to ensure complete removal of the template reagent.

The nanosensor was prepared by adding 10 mg of the probe to 10 mL of ethanol that contained 100 mg of SiCDs@MSN. The mixture was sonicated for 1 h and then evaporated under reduced pressure. This process was repeated thrice. The nanosensor was then washed with ethanol and collected via centrifugation.

The weight amount of probe in supernatant could be calculated based on the standard curve derived from the absorption spectrum of the different concentration of probe solution. The probe loading efficiency was calculated as follow: probe loading efficiency (wt%) = (original probe - probe in supernatant) ÷ (original probe) × 100%.

A series of solutions with different concentrations was prepared by adding different amounts of Cys to phosphate-buffered saline solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM). After the incubation of different concentrations of Cys and nanosensor (0.5 mg/mL) solutions in phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) for 10 min, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Selectivity experiments were performed under the same conditions and operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC experiments were performed with acetonitrile: 0.1% acetic acid solution (10 : 90, v/v) as mobile phase at room temperature. Human serum was diluted with HPLC grade water for 10 times before delivered. Five concentrations (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 mM) of Cys solution were used as standard to draw a standard curve. The Cys concentration in human serum was calculated according to the standard curve. For the detection of Cys using the nanosensor, the serum samples were diluted 200-fold with PBS (pH 7.4, 10 mM). The Cys concentration of the serum samples was optically detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, the TEM image proved that SiCDs were monodispersed in water and had an average diameter of 4.1 nm. The presence of various functional groups in the SiCDs was confirmed via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra exhibited several distinct absorption peaks that range from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The absorbance peak at 912 cm−1 was ascribed to the vibrational stretching of the Si–N bond. The stretching vibration of Si–O occurred at 1150 cm−1. The absorbance peak at 1386 cm−1 was attributed to C–H bending. The absorbance at 2935 cm−1 was assigned to the O–H stretching vibration. The strong signals at 1583 cm−1 and 3365 cm−1 were attributed to the N–H bending vibration and the N–H stretching vibration, respectively [41–44]. The FTIR results demonstrated that the resultant SiCDs had abundant amino groups. Moreover, XPS measurement was performed to determine the chemical compositions of the as-prepared SiCDs. Full-range XPS analysis of the SiCDs showed five peaks at 101.6, 153.1, 284.6, 399.0, and 532.4 eV, which corresponded to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 2 A). High-resolution C 1s analysis revealed that four types of carbon atoms emanated from C–Si (284.0 eV), C–C (284.8 eV), C–N/C–O (285.6 eV), and CO (286.9 eV) (Fig. 2B). The three fitted peaks at 398.5, 399.1, and 399.9 eV in the N 1s spectrum were ascribed to the N–Si, C–N–C, and N–H groups, respectively (Fig. 2C). This result indicated that the surfaces of the as-prepared SiCDs were rich in amino groups and was consistent with the corresponding FTIR spectrum. The O 1s spectrum indicated that the peaks at 531.0, 531.9, and 532.7 eV were for CO, C–OH/C–O–C, and Si–O, respectively (Fig. 2D). The Si 2p peaks at 101.1, 101.9, and 102.7 eV could be attributed to Si–C, Si–N, and Si–O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2E) [2,44–46]. The surface components of the SiCDs determined by the XPS were in good agreement with FTIR results.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the SiCDs in aqueous solution (0.07 mg/mL) were investigated (Fig. 1C and D). Two typical absorption bands centered at 240 and 363 nm were observed in the UV–vis spectrum and were assigned to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition, respectively [43,47–49]. The fluorescence emission peak of the SiCDs appeared at 450 nm when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the SiCDs at various excitation wavelengths (350–420 nm) are shown in Fig. 1D. The insert corresponds to the normalized fluorescence spectrum. The fluorescence spectra of the SiCDs were independent of the excitation wavelength, indicating the absence of size-dependent emission behavior. Furthermore, the fluorescent quantum yield of the as-prepared SiCDs was 72% with quinine sulfate (ΦF = 0.54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference (Figure S1D) [50].

The stability of the as-prepared SiCDs was tested to investigate their performance further. The fluorescent intensity of the SiCDs under extreme pH, strong light exposure, and high ionic strengths in solution was measured to investigate stability. The SiCDs had strong and relatively stable fluorescent activities within the pH range of 4–13 (Figure S1A). However, when pH was 2 and 3, the fluorescent intensity of the SiCDs weakened. This effect may be related to the presence of abundant amino groups on the surfaces of the SiCDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the SiCDs remained stable despite being exposed to light illumination at 365 nm for 60 min. This result suggested that they had excellent photostability (Figure S1B). The fluorescence intensity of the SiCDs remained almost constant until the concentration of the NaCl solution reached 100 mM similar to that in the solution without NaCl, indicating the outstanding stability of the SiCDs under highly ionic conditions (Figure S1C). The aforementioned results suggested that the SiCDs were sufficiently stable in ambient environmental conditions.

SiCDs@MSN was synthesized via a one-step emulsion method. CTAB was used as an emulsifier and template reagent. The morphology of the as-prepared SiCDs@MSN was characterized via TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the SiCDs@MSN exhibited a monodispersed morphology with a uniform spherical structure. The mean diameter of the SiCDs@MSN was approximately 50 nm (Fig. 3B).

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms showed that the adsorption capacity of the nanosensor was lower than that of unloaded SiCDs@MSN (Fig. 3C and D). The BET surface area of the nanosensor ranged from 767.4 cm3 g−1 to 896.6 cm3 g−1 (Table S1). Some small organic molecules may have adsorbed on the surface of the as-prepared nanosensor, thereby increasing the surface area of the nanosensor. The calculated volume of the pores ranged from 1.853 cm3 g−1 to 1.704 cm3 g−1 (Table S1), suggesting that the probe was successfully loaded into the mesopores and the loading efficiency of probe was about 20 wt% (Figure S4).

The stability of the nanosensor under different pH and ionic strengths in solution was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor remained almost constant until the concentration of the NaCl solution reached 100 mM, indicating the outstanding stability of the nanosensor under highly ionic conditions (Figure S3A). The nanosensor had strong and relatively stable fluorescent activities within the pH range of 4–13 (Figure S3B). However, when pH was 2 and 3, the fluorescent intensity of the nanosensor weakened. The structure of nanosensor may be damaged in strong acid, which leads to the fluorescent quenching of nanosensor. These results suggested that the nanosensor were enough stable in ambient environmental conditions.

Fluorescence detection was performed in PBS (pH 7.4, 10 mM). As inferred from the emission spectra of the SiCDs and the emission spectra of the probe before and after Cys treatment (Figure S2), the probe had negligible absorbance within the region of 400–540 nm and was practically nonfluorescent in the visible region. After Cys treatment, the probe transformed into substances with an intense green fluorescence and an absorbance overlapping the blue fluorescence emission of the SiCDs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the SiCDs at 450 nm negligibly changed. However, it still exhibited some changes and can be used as an internal reference. By contrast, the emission of carboxyfluorescein at 518 nm increased as Cys concentrations increased. It was reported that the cleavage of acrylic ester under Cys treatment was very efficient because of the high nucleophilicity of thiol group and the tendency to form a 7-membered ring [51]. In the presence of Cys, the probe formed fluorescein by the cleavage of an ester bond, resulting in fluorescence.

The amount of Cys was quantitatively evaluated by obtaining a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.991) for Cys concentrations ranging from 0 μM to 90 μM by plotting the I518/I450 ratio versus Cys concentration (Fig. 4B). The detection limit of Cys was 0.73 μM on the basis of 3δ/S, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and S is the slope of the calibration graph. A comparison of the analytical performance of this method and that of recently reported assays is summarized in Table S2. The method proposed herein had a wide linear range and a lower detection limit, indicating a great promise for high-performance detection of Cys.

The selectivity of the nanosensor for Cys was evaluated by examining the fluorescence intensity ratio (I518/I450) of the nanosensor in the presence of other amino acids, such as aspartic acid (Asp), GSH, glycine (Gly), Hcy, and methionine (Met) under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , the influence of these interferents on the Cys detection performance of the nanosensor was negligible. These results implied that the prepared SiCDs-based nanohybrid system may be used as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cys detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing system proposed herein was used for the detection of Cys in human serum to evaluate its accuracy and reliability under real conditions. By converting the fluorescence intensity ratio (Figure S5) to concentration using the characteristic equation, Cys in serum was found to be 64.6 μM in 200 times diluted serum, which means that Cys concentration in original serum sample was 12.92 mM. By comparison, HPLC analysis was carried as standard. Concentration of Cys in human serum sample was found to be 12.33 mM (Figure S5). These results are listed in Table S3. The Cys level measured by nanosensor is higher than the result by HPLC. The possible reason may be that the nanosensor was interfered by some biomolecules triggering false positive. This concentration is much higher than reported healthy range, which may be caused by the hydrolysis of protein and peptide in improper storage [52]. The result indicated that the ratiometric nanosensor could be applied for Cys detection in clinical diagnosis.

A ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for Cys was designed and prepared by loading probes on CDs-doped mesoporous silicon particles. This approach could effectively eliminate the potential influence from background fluorescence and other analyte-independent external environmental factors via the ratiometric method. The nanosensor exhibited the characteristic emission of the probe and SiCDs upon the addition of Cys at a single excitation wavelength. Cys concentration could be calculated on the basis of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I518/I450) by using the fitted equation. The selectivity assay revealed that the ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor had a better selectivity for Cys than the other biological thiols. The limit of detection was 0.73 μM. Moreover, the ratiometric nanosensor could be used to detect Cys in human serum. Therefore, the system proposed herein implemented as a new strategy for detecting Cys.

Huajuan Deng, designed this work, performed part of experiments, analyzed data and wrote paper. Zitong Wu, performed probe synthesis and provided serum. Zexu Zhao, performed fluorescence properties of SiCDs testing. Lin zhu, performed SiCDs morphology characterization. Minggen Tang, performed SiCDs-doped mesoporous silicon and characterization. Ruijin Yu, revised the paper. Jinyi Wang, revised the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675126 and 21874108).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aconitic acid derived carbon dots: conjugated interaction for the detection of folic acid and fluorescence targeted imaging of folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells
